family_audit: accepted_primary: PTX export-surface cleanup on the restored accepted PTX branch
accepted_secondary: bounded PTX control-path exploit now that the grouping window is back on the accepted surface
accepted_tertiary: grouped-row sibling export-scratch trim as an off-branch fallback family
deferred: repeating another grouping-window restore immediately after the round-6 win
rejected_primary: the 256x128 pivot family remains negative for this loop

directions.name: Apply Only A Minimal PTX Export Address Cleanup
directions.hypothesis: Round 6 recovered the accepted PTX grouping surface and produced a clear runtime win, but the restored profile still carries the same occupancy ceiling and modest barrier / scoreboard costs. That points to the remaining overhead sitting close to the PTX export path rather than in another dispatch or locality knob. The best bounded next move is therefore to keep the restored grouping window intact and simplify only the PTX export address/control surface so writeback bookkeeping dies sooner without changing the MMA loop or widening the hot path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX export-side address/control overhead and scratch bookkeeping on the restored accepted hot-band surface.
directions.risk: Low to medium. This keeps the freshly restored PTX branch intact and only narrows the export surface, but the upside is bounded and it can still become a no-op if the compiler already simplified the current path.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.180656 ms restored base
ranking_notes: The restore family already paid off, so the next step should harvest that restored surface rather than immediately twiddle grouping again.
Export cleanup is the best-ranked still-open family that stays on the new accepted PTX branch and attacks a different surface from round 5 and round 6.
It is ranked ahead of the control-path exploit because the last direct control-order nudge already came back flat on the adjacent surface.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if export cleanup leaves runtime and the export-adjacent stall counters effectively unchanged after one bounded pass.

directions.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
directions.hypothesis: Now that round 6 restored the accepted grouping window, the still-open PTX control-path family becomes coherent again as a secondary option. The earlier flat result came from a B-first prologue retune on the wrong surface; on the restored grouping base, a different bounded control/export nudge could still matter. This family stays below dir_01 because it already consumed one round for almost no gain, but it remains a plausible live-branch follow-up rather than a closed family.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead on the restored grouping surface, not another locality or macro-tiling miss.
directions.risk: Moderate. This family is still live, but another too-small control-order nudge can easily collapse back into noise.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.180656 ms restored base
ranking_notes: The family is still at the top of the persistent frontier, but it is no longer the first pick after the round-5 flat result.
Keeping it ranked second preserves an actionable live-branch alternative without pretending the first control-order tweak was a success.
If export cleanup is flat too, this family can still absorb another bounded attempt on the restored base.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if another bounded control-path tweak again behaves like noise on top of the restored grouping surface.

directions.name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To The PTX-Style Single Stage
directions.hypothesis: The live search queue should still keep one off-branch export family active so the loop does not collapse entirely onto the PTX hot path. The strongest recovered candidate there is the grouped-row sibling export-scratch trim, which historically measured 24.422464 ms. It stays low-ranked because it leaves the restored PTX winner, but it is still the best non-PTX export-side family worth preserving in the set.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Export scratch lifetime and writeback overhead on the grouped-row 128x128 sibling path, used as an off-branch fallback family.
directions.risk: Low to medium. The family has historical evidence and bounded scope, but it requires leaving the restored PTX path that just won.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.180656 ms restored base
ranking_notes: This keeps one credible off-branch export family alive after the round-history queue rehydration.
It is deliberately below the two PTX-local families because the restored PTX path just won and should be exploited first.
If the PTX-local cleanup families flatten out, this branch becomes the cleanest next escape hatch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the sibling export-scratch trim cannot beat its own historical 24.422464 ms band or if switching off the restored PTX path immediately gives the win back.

notes: Round 7/100 audit: restoring the historical PTX grouping window and accepted-surface prologue delivered a real win, dropping runtime from 24.535040 ms to 24.180656 ms. That says the search should bank the restored surface and spend the next budget unit on a new family rather than immediately iterate the same restore knob again. The restored run still shows the familiar signature: 16.62% active warps, register-limited occupancy, barrier 5.48%, and long-scoreboard 7.17% with low DRAM pressure. That makes the PTX export-address/control surface the best next bounded family, with a second PTX control-path exploit and one off-branch export-scratch family kept alive behind it.
